sources.notes: Joseph Platt entry confirms children Hannah (m. Fletcher Newton) and Phebe (m. Samuel Newton)

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters each married a Newton first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: His daughters Phebe and Hannah both married into the Newton family of Milford — Phebe to Samuel Newton, Hannah to Samuel's first cousin Fletcher Newton. Two Platt sisters from the same household, two Newton cousins. Joseph was himself the son of Deacon Richard Platt, one of Milford's founding settlers.

narrative_blocks.header: At dead of night he and his friends
narrative_blocks.body: In 1671, young Joseph Platt and companions George Clark, Joseph Northrup, and Samuel Clark destroyed the Wepawaug Indians' fort "with the utmost secrecy, at dead of night." The fort was probably vacant. They were tried before the General Court at New Haven, each fined £10, and ordered to rebuild what they had torn down.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant of the Milford Train Band, 1698
narrative_blocks.body: Trainbands were the basic colonial militia unit — periodic training companies from which fighting forces were selected. He was Lieutenant of the Eldest Train Band at Milford in October 1698, and was elected Deputy from Milford to the Connecticut General Assembly in May 1700.

narrative_blocks.header: No will — his son Joseph administered
narrative_blocks.body: No will of Joseph is on record. His inventory was taken March 21, 1703/4. His widow Mary and son Joseph were appointed administrators, though the son was still a minor at the time.

cross_connections.display_label: Both destroyed the Wepawaug fort — fined £10.

sources.notes: Joseph Platt entry confirms children Hannah (m. Fletcher Newton) and Phebe (m. Samuel Newton)

Two Platt sisters from the same household, two Newton cousins. Joseph was himself the son of Deacon Richard Platt, one of Milford's founding settlers.

The fort was probably vacant. They were tried before the General Court at New Haven, each fined £10, and ordered to rebuild what they had torn down.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant of the Milford Train Band, 1698
narrative_blocks.body: Trainbands were the basic colonial militia unit — periodic training companies from which fighting forces were selected. He was Lieutenant of the Eldest Train Band at Milford in October 1698, and was elected Deputy from Milford to the Connecticut General Assembly in May 1700.

narrative_blocks.body: No will of Joseph is on record. His inventory was taken March 21, 1703/4. His widow Mary and son Joseph were appointed administrators, though the son was still a minor at the time.

cross_connections.display_label: Both destroyed the Wepawaug fort — fined £10.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters each married a Newton first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughters Phebe and Hannah both married into the Newton family — Phebe to Samuel Newton, Hannah to Samuel's first cousin Fletcher Newton. Two sisters, two cousins, all from Milford.

narrative_blocks.header: The whole family entered the church covenant
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, she and Samuel were admitted to full communion in the Milford church on the same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sister Hannah married her husband's first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe married Samuel Newton; her sister Hannah married Samuel's first cousin Fletcher Newton. Two Platt sisters wed two Newton first cousins — the families of Milford knit together across one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Whole family entered the church covenant together
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, Samuel and Phebe were admitted to full communion in the Milford church — on the very same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Newton cousins married two Platt sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Phebe Platt in Milford; his first cousin Fletcher Newton married Phebe's sister Hannah Platt. Two Newton cousins took two Platt sisters — the families of Milford interlacing through a single generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His first cousin — both married Platt sisters from the same Milford family.
His great-great-grandson married a Hooker descendant — confirmed double Hooker

cross_connections.notes: Their son Jonah Jr. (HD0081) thus has descent_paths_to_thomas unchanged but double Platt ancestry.
cross_connections.display_label: First-cousin marriage to fellow Platt grandchild

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Phebe Platt
narrative_blocks.body: He married Phebe Platt, his first cousin: his own mother was Phebe Platt and his wife was Phebe Platt, both granddaughters of Joseph Platt of Milford. The two women shared their grandfather, their first name, and their last name.

narrative_blocks.header: Died three days after his wife
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe died April 26, 1756; Jonah died three days later on April 29. Their son Jonah Jr. was about eleven years old. Edward Hooker records both dates in the same entry without comment on the coincidence.

narrative_blocks.header: Tombstone rank 'Sergt.' — daughter went to Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: His tombstone identifies him by the military rank 'Sergt.' — sergeant. His son Jonah Jr. was eleven when he died; his daughter Phebe later married Oliver Sanford and removed to Poultney, Vermont.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at ten years old
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Newton died on February 20, 1729, at about ten years old.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sister Phebe married her husband's first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah married Fletcher Newton; her sister Phebe married Fletcher's first cousin Samuel Newton. Two Platt sisters wed two Newton first cousins in the same generation — tightening the bonds of two old Milford families.

sources.notes: Caroline Newton genealogy — includes map of Durham land plots
Death year 1750 confirmed by tombstone inscription (TSM = Tombstone in Milford, per Caroline Newton abbreviation system)

narrative_blocks.header: His gravestone reads: "Memento Mori"
narrative_blocks.body: His grave in the Milford cemetery carries a Latin reminder: "Memento Mori — Here lyes buryed the body of Lieut. Fletcher Newton." He died around 1750, about three years after drafting his will.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin daughters born 1718
narrative_blocks.body: Alice and Hannah, twins born December 1718, had different paths: Alice died unmarried in 1777; Hannah married John Prudden and carried the line forward.

narrative_blocks.header: First cousin Samuel also married Hannah's sister Phebe
narrative_blocks.body: Fletcher married Hannah Platt; his first cousin Samuel Newton married Hannah's sister Phebe Platt. Two Newton cousins and two Platt sisters — all from Milford — bound the families together across a single generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His first cousin — both married Platt sisters from the same Milford family.

cross_connections.notes: Joanna Prudden (widow of Rev. Peter Prudden) married Thomas Willett Sep 20, 1671.
cross_connections.display_label: Her husband's Prudden ancestor's widow married Mary Willet's father

cross_connections.notes: Joanna Prudden (widow of Rev. Peter Prudden) married Thomas Willett Sep 20, 1671.
cross_connections.display_label: Her husband's family Prudden line connects to his Willet wife

cross_connections.notes: Joanna Prudden (widow of Rev. Peter Prudden) married Thomas Willett Sep 20, 1671.
cross_connections.display_label: He married Joanna Prudden — her husband's ancestral family

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized with her twin sister Alice
narrative_blocks.body: She was baptized December 14, 1718 in Milford, twin to her sister Alice. The Newton twins were part of the fourth-generation cluster of Milford Newtons descended from Rev. Roger Newton Sr. and Mary Hooker Newton.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into a family in the Hooker tree
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband John Prudden was son of John Prudden of Milford. The Prudden family went back to Rev. Peter Prudden, first minister of Milford, whose widow Joanna Prudden later married Thomas Willett — father of Mary Willet Hooker, wife of Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Elizabeth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth died young; the year was not recorded.

death.cause: Died Milford shortly before Feb 13, 1684/5, when inventory of estate taken. Burial place unknown — probably in his orchard.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Milford Cemetery commemorates him. Actual burial place unknown; probably in his orchard per family tradition.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary (maiden name unknown), wife of George Platt
parents.father_research_notes: George Platt of Ware (bpt May 13, 1582; d. Apr 20, 1609) — tailor

migration.departure: England 1637-1638 — sailed with Rev. Peter Prudden's Hertfordshire company
migration.notes: Sold his lands and houses at Ware by final concord at Westminster Apr 25, 1638. Was among the 66 men who formed themselves into a church society at New Haven under Rev. Peter Prudden Aug 22, 1639, which group then settled Milford. Enrolled among first settlers of Milford Nov 20, 1639, with four in his family. Home lot #38: 4 acres 1 rod near present West Main and Cherry Streets, Milford.

church_roles.notes: Chosen deacon 1669. Witnessed the will of Rev. Peter Prudden.

cross_connections.display_label: Both came from Hertfordshire; both among Milford's Seven Pillars.
Adjoining home lots in the Hertfordshire Quarter, New Haven.
led the Cambridge circle Platt neighbored in Hertfordshire

notable_persons.name: Ben Affleck
notable_persons.description: Actor and director (Good Will Hunting, Batman v Superman, Gone Girl). Descended from Richard Platt through a daughter who married and lost the Platt surname — six generations documented on PBS Finding Your Roots (Season 3, October 2016), hosted by Henry Louis Gates Jr.
notable_persons.source: PBS Finding Your Roots Season 3; confirmed by Milford genealogist Richard Platt and Alvin Platt's 2003 Special Compilation of the History and Genealogy of Richard Platt
notable_persons.notes: Gates presented Affleck with six generations of Platt charts on air. The Milford Richard Platt Society confirmed the identification matches their documented records.

narrative_blocks.header: He left a Bible to each
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Richard Platt made his will in 1683, he bequeathed a Bible to each of his nineteen grandchildren — a gesture that, as one historian noted, "betrays a profound interest in the best things in life" in rough pioneer days. His estate was valued at approximately £600 sterling.

narrative_blocks.header: From Rev. Chauncey's congregation in Ware to founding
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Platt grew up in Ware, Hertfordshire — a hotbed of Puritanism where the famous Rev. Charles Chauncey (later president of Harvard) preached from 1627 to 1633. Deeply influenced by Chauncey's teaching, he came to New England in 1638 with Rev. Peter Prudden's Hertfordshire company, and became one of 66 founding members of the Milford church on August 22, 1639.

narrative_blocks.header: His name is on Milford's Founder's Bridge
narrative_blocks.body: The Platt stone on the Wepawaug Memorial Bridge in Milford reads: "Deacon Richard Platt, Obit 1684 — Mary His Wife." He was chosen Deacon of First Church Milford in 1669, witnessed the will of Rev. Peter Prudden, and appears as a witness in the colony records. He is buried in his orchard at lot #38, West Main and Cherry Streets, Milford.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children — their descendants married throughout
narrative_blocks.body: His nine children — Mary, John, Isaac, Samuel, Sarah, Epenetus, Hannah, Josiah, and Joseph — spread through the families of Milford and surrounding towns. Daughter Mary married Luke Atkinson; daughter Sarah married Thomas Beach then Miles Merwin; son Joseph married Mary Kellogg. Their descendants appear as spouses throughout the Newton-Hooker family network across three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: He left his entire estate to son Joseph
narrative_blocks.body: His will left Joseph Platt the entirety of his estate: "I give my son Joseph Platt all the remainder of my estate... I make my son Joseph Platt my executor." The family had emigrated from Hertfordshire, England in 1639, helping found New Haven and Milford. Joseph was the first Platt child born in America.

death.cause: Buried Milford March 24, 1675/6. Admitted to Milford church Aug 15, 1641.

crossConnections.display_label: Both destroyed the Wepawaug fort — fined £10.
